speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed Swenson describes more about the early life of Park, clearing, harvesting and other homestead activities, building the Lutheran Church and celebrating Christmas playing in the winter. He tales of the wilderness country around Park, of fishing with spears, hunting and cougar and of hauling supplies to an isolated mining camp near Elk River. He speaks of troubles he had with drunks and saloons during the railroad building days and of the time of saloons in early Troy. Ed also explains how he decided to leave Park in 1910 and how the area changed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was wondering about, was just a sort of an overall feeling for what families did to get by in a homesteading?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, most of them, the men would work out in harvest out on the prairie, you know, through the harvest season. And then in the late fall and winter, they would be clearing more land and get land broke up, you know, to raise little grain and hay. They'd keep a few head of cattle and so they'd have a few to sell, you know. It took a few years before they got a threshing outfit to thresh their grain, so they cut most of it for hay until that time. And there were five of the neighbors there that sort of formed a company that went out and bought a used threshing outfit out in the Genesee Valley. It was a horsepower outfit, brought that home.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They formed a crew for threshing and exchanged work with each other so nobody had much of a thresh bill, you know. And then they would hold wheat out to the flour mills and get flour to last through the year. What more they had, they would sell then to the warehouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was water much of a problem?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, we had lots of snow in the winter in those times. There were springs that would, towards fall, they would get pretty low, but nearly everybody had water fairly close to the buildings. There was only one family I knew of in there that had to haul water in the summer.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Go on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had to haul water?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They had a water tank and they hauled with wagons and horses, you know, up to the house. They had a cistern there that they would empty into. But during the wet season, they nearly all had plenty of water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the water coming from wells or did it from springs?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Springs, yeah. Or small creeks, you know, that would run through the summer or the year round. They had to carry it. Sometimes they had to carry it maybe a couple hundred yards or so, but they were used to hardships. As long as there was plenty of water there, we had to carry water about, I'd say about a hundred yards or something like that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We dug a cistern and lined it up with rocks and hauled water into that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So we had water close to the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just used the cistern for storing water that you hauled from your spring? You used the cistern to store water that you hauled from your spring?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the work that your mother did? What kind of jobs did she do to keep the family going?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: She was at home all the time, did the housework. We had big gardens and a big orchard. We had close to two acres of fruit orchard. So she did a lot of canning. Of course, we had plenty of milk and cream and she made butter, stored butter for the winter. We had a good cold cellar, so she put up a lot of fruit and butter and things like that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They salted meat in a big barrel when we butchered in the fall. We'd have plenty of meat and fruit. We had chickens and a couple of pigs every fall. So we had plenty of meat. And the neighbors the same way, of course. When butchering time came in the fall, two or three neighbors get together and they'd butcher in one place and then they'd go to the next one. So they all had their help free of charge.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Until the children began to grow up and be helped.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I know we boys stayed at home until we were grown up before we tried to get out on our own. I stayed up there. I was the youngest of the boys, so I stayed home to do the work there and take care of the folks when they got old.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You mentioned this cooperating several times. I get the idea that there was a lot of cooperation between the two. Oh yes, that was a general rule there. When you needed help, you call on some neighbor that would help. Then you'd go and help him back again. There was no money exchange on that. No matter what kind of work it was, they were all familiar with it. And they all had the same kind of work practically.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: In breaking up land, someone had a good big breaking plow and then if more horses were needed, the neighbor would add on the team and you'd break a while on yours and then go and break for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this breaking a very hard work? Because I was wondering with the meadow being sort of meadowy, if that would make it easier than some places to break land?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, the hard part about it was that by that time, this service brush had started growing up and those roots were kind of hard to break through. So it was slow work.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And we didn't have a good grade of horses at that time. They were a mixed breed of horses and not very big horses. An average farm horse in those times would be between ten hundred and twelve hundred pounds. And that isn't a very big horse. Later on they began to get better horses and do better work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father have horses when he started?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, it took a couple of years. The first thing he got was milk cows because that was part of our living. And then my oldest brother had horses and he would help. Then another neighbor that Father Exchange worked with, he had four pretty good horses. So he would come and break land for us and we would help him clear land or something like that to pay back.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Until we had horses of our own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember the order of things that were important to your father?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You said that you built the cabin first. That was the first thing to do. And then you wanted to get a milk cow right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What other things were the most important things to get done on the homestead?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, after we got the house up and moved in, he began to cut logs for a barn. There were three or four men together then to build the barn up because they were longer and heavier logs.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: As soon as they got the barn up and had a place for the cows, then they built a chicken house. And then was the cellar and next was a woodshed. So there was a lot of work to do.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The first and second year we were there, he didn't have anything broke up the first year. The second year he got a few acres broke up. And it happened, there was an Indian trail through our place from down at Lapway. They used to come up through the summer and go hunting and fishing back in the woods, you know, and pick berries and take home. So they would come across there until we had the land sand thin.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And this trail that they had across there was quite close to our buildings. Probably something like 30 or 40 yards from the house. And they had a camping place there where they would stop and eat before they went into the mountains.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So the second summer after we moved in, they came through there and they stopped and camped there and went on. The third summer we had some land broke up and that took part of that trail they had through there. And Father had sowed some oats then for hay. The oats were up a few inches and the Indians came and they didn't seem to understand this. They used the same camping place to take the horses out in the oat field. Father and the older boys were away that day and Mother was worried about them destroying the oat ground there. So she told me I better go over and tell them to move their horses. Well I was afraid of Indians, you know, after hearing all the old battles they had with them. But I got enough courage to go over there and of course I couldn't speak their language but I managed some way by pointing and talking that they should move the horses over on the grass instead of. And they were friendly, they agreed. They went and pulled the stakes up and moved the horses. I went back to the house. Well Mother saw that they were friendly so she said you go and tell them to come to the house to eat their dinner, their lunch. I don't know how I managed that but somehow or other they understood and they came to the house. Mother had moved the table out to the middle of the floor so they could sit around. There was six or eight of them. They came in and they just took the table and moved it back to where it was before and they sat around in a circle on the floor like they would in their camps, you know. And had their food with them. Well Mother had coffee made and she put the coffee pot down on the floor among them there and brought sugar and cream and a little fruit and things like that for them. And they enjoyed that and they chatted away and enjoyed themselves. Then they went away and in the fall, early fall they came back through and they stopped and they would buy deer hides. They could tan deer hides and make mogginsons and things like that. So they stopped to buy deer hides. Well we had one deer hide and it happened to be a nice big one. And the price that they paid ordinarily was fifty cents for a deer hide. But Mother thought that was a pretty nice deer hide so she wanted more. So she somehow or other made them understand she wanted seventy -five cents. Then they talked among themselves a while and finally agreed to give her seventy -five cents for the deer hide. And after that, they didn't come then after they had fenced our landing, but we knew if they came anywhere through there, if we had deer hides, that's why we'd get in touch with them and sell the deer hide because they were no good to us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were the same Indians that came back that were there the first time?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well we had a kind of a road made then, you know, through the settlement towards the mountains so they would come along that road. And that was only a quarter of a mile from our house. So we knew when they came I would go where they can't and make a deal with them. Of course it was the older boys that did that. I wasn't old enough to go hunting or that, but the older boys would manage that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about your own going fishing and hunting?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well I didn't go until I was around twelve, thirteen years old. By that time, father stayed home then. He was getting a little older, so it was just the older boys and I. Two of my brothers and I were the only boys at home with the folks then. So we would go back there, horseback, go back there and hunt. There was one place on what was called the Long Meadow where there were four big spruce trees in a kind of a group. And they were big trees and it was always cool and nice around among those trees. So we formed a camp there, just a small fire to do our cooking with. So that was called the camp. Everybody knew where that was. So when they go up there to fish or so, they'd bring their camp tools along and put it by those trees. So we had a nice place there to camp, stay overnight there when it wasn't storming. And there was still snow in the woods there, there was still snow. So when we caught salmon, why, we'd bury them in the snow to keep them fresh until we came home. So that was the change. After having venison so much, we'd have salmon and then there was patridge and grouse in the woods there. So we'd go and get some of them once in a while, have a variety of meat, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you catch the salmon? What did you use?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Spears for salmon. A neighbor of ours had a little blacksmith shop and he was handy with that kind of work and he made spears for some of the neighbors around there. And you put a long handle on that like you would a fork handle, except it would be longer. And you could wade out close to the edge and stand and wait until some of them come by and then you'd grab them with that spear.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And dress them up and put them in the snow bank until we either wanted some of it to eat or keep until we go home. We'd stay in there a couple days or so, you know, each time. And in summer there was trout in there, mountain trout. So then we'd go in there with hook and line and catch trout, you know. Have our frying pans and stuff along and have trout meals there too and bring home some. So that was our vacation, you might call it, for the first few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it feel to be a boy and growing up in that country?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We really enjoyed it. My brother older than I, that is four years older, we were together a lot, you know. We'd go in the summer, we'd go back in the woods and pick berries and shoot grouse and pheasants and climb trees. We'd climb a lot of trees there when we were growing up. And then of course we'd help father with the haying and do what we could when he was building something. And he would split rails and posts and then we'd help with the fencing, what we could. And my brother of course, he was soon old enough to do quite a bit of work.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And before we got any land broke up for hay, the wild grass grew tall in the woods and out in the open. There were no cattle running on the ranch there, you know, so the grass grew. And father had a good sight and he was pretty handy with that. And he would cut grass around in the openings and my brother and I would follow with a rake a piece and rake it up with handrakes, you know. And then get the wagon and haul it home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That made good feed for the cows?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, it wasn't quite as good as oat hay would be, but we had lots of it. We had only two cows for several years. So we had plenty of it. I know he had built a barn attached to the stable and we had quite a lot of hay up in there. One winter we were in short of hay and the cows liked the moss that grew on the trees, you know. So when the snow wasn't deep, when we turned them out, they'd go out there and eat moss. And then they'd have hay in the mangers in the evening again and some in the morning. When the snow got deep, I see father and my next older brother. He was about 18 years old. Second year we were there. So he and father would fall trees and my brother Adolf and I, we took gunner sacks and went up there and picked moss and put it in the sacks and carried it down to the hay mow and put it in there. It was only just a short time until spring came. Snow went down and the cows would be out in the woods and forage around. So we managed somewhat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When would your winter come in those days? When would the winter come and snow come to stay?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, the latter part of October.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We would get around three feet of snow during the winter. But very little drifting because there was so much timber further west where most of the wind came. So we didn't have any trouble with snowdrifts to speak of. But we had to shovel snow to get out to the road, to the other road, when the snow got deep, to get out to town.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Troy was our shopping place then. We'd leave home about three o 'clock in the morning with Tim and his sled. We'd get to Troy about ten o 'clock and let the horses eat and rest until about four in the afternoon. So they were rested for the trip back. We'd get home about ten o 'clock at night. But then after that the horses wouldn't have work to do then for some time so they had time to rest up again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which road was this that you were taking over?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well that would be the second road that was built across the canyon. The first road didn't amount to anything. The second road, it took about a whole summer to build that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, are you telling me about the building?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We would go out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, tell me, what did Troy look like to you as a boy?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, Troy was first built up only about a year before we came to Idaho. So there was almost nothing there. But there was a grocery store so the folks could buy what they needed. And so they didn't go to Troy very often. But like in the fall, shortly before Christmas, they'd go out and supply up for the winter, you know. With groceries and things. And we had only a coal oil lamp for lights in the house. So they'd buy a five gallon can of coal oil, or kerosene they called it. And that would do for light for most of the winter.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And my folks had learned to card and spin wool. They would buy the wool and clean it up and card it. And Mother had a spinning wheel so she would spin the yarn. And she knew how to do knitting, you know. So she would knit socks and mittens for us kids that we had for the winter. When the outside work was done, then both Father and Mother would card the wool. They had two sets of cards, they called it. That's a rig you put the wool on and it has fine teeth on it about half inch long. And they would lay the wool and take a handful or so and spread it out on the one card. Have it turned up and then they'd turn the other card down and pull them back and forth. Like if you were combing hair or something. Get the wool all straightened up into nice bunches for spinning then, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And Father's work and with us boys we would cut down trees and cut up for wood for the year during the winter. So we had none of that to do in the summer.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: When I got old enough to help with the crosscut saw, then my brother Adolph and I would do the sawing and Father split it into wood, you know. Kitchen wood. We had just the one stove, a cook stove in the old house. That was big enough to heat the house because it was only one room coming until we got the other house up.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And Father was pretty good at making shakes and shingles. He had a rig to shave that with so we would go out and cut cedars and cut into blocks and bring it home and he set up a place in the woodshed. So that was part of his winter's work then. And he made shingles and shakes for some of the neighbors. So he was always busy through the winter. When we didn't make fence rails and posts, he'd be making shakes and wood for the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much free time to be playing? What kind of play did you do besides hunting and fishing?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, Father made skis for us. He cut down the fir trees just about right size for say about four inches in diameter. And he'd split them and hew them down pretty well and then he'd use that knife he used for making shakes and he'd shave them down to just about right size for skis. And he had a way of bending the front end to make the front end on the skis. The wood would be green when he first made them and then he would heat them on a fire outside. And he had a way of making a bend in them with some sticks and a pole. I can't tell you just how, but he made so the front end was bowed up on them. And then he put the straps in for our shoes, you know. So then we'd go skiing. There was a pretty good sized hill on one part of our place and we got that cleared up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Down the hill that was near home?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Some of the neighbors had skis and they would come over there and then we'd have a ski run, you know, with. And they'd build up snow mounds there to ski over and that would make a sort of a jump off of there. And they'd build up higher and higher and finally they built up so they had one part way down the hill and another one a little further down the hill. And you could ride the first one pretty good, but if you didn't have good balance when you hit the next one you'd land up on your neck and shoulders in the snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you sled too?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He made a sled, a coasting sled. It was about probably five feet long so there was room for two or three of them there. And we used that like my younger sister and I before we were able to try and ski and, you know, we would have that sled to ride on.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And finally, as we got older, she would get tired of skiing so then we boys would get on there, about three of us, and it got to be a little too much for the sled. And we landed up breaking one of the runners on it. So that ended that. But we had the skis until we plowed the land up and we got tired of the skis and we had saddle horses instead.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So you rode around a lot too for your pleasure on saddle horses.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes, we, as boys, we had fixed up so we had race ground over there, you know, one good level stretch of road where we'd go and have races once in a while. Of course there wasn't much money involved but there was lots of fun in it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And then we'd go horseback to town if there wasn't much to carry. We'd rather go horseback than drive a team, you know. We used the horses a lot.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Then we took to going further away on hunting trips where the land wasn't settled up so much. We'd have a pack horse and two, three saddle horses and go back up towards Boville and up in that part and go camping.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: My oldest sister and her family came west one fall and next spring when the snow went off we packed up for a hunting trip back towards St. Mary's up in there. He had never camped out like that because they were on the prairie back east. So he had quite an experience. And we, one day we were camped there and in the afternoon we were going to catch some fish for, fry for the supper. And while we got ready to fish a snowstorm came up and he thought that was quite a thing to see, catching a fish and have it land in a snowstorm. So he had that to tell when he got back east. And one night the wind came up and a dead pine tree was blown down and on top of that was only just a few steps from our camp. So we were more careful in choosing a place to camp after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did any people around park get lost in the woods? Was there ever trouble with anybody getting lost in the woods?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was only once one of our neighbors. He got lost.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He didn't want to fire any shots for help because he didn't have much ammunition with him. So he just sat down and waited. When he didn't come home the children got worried. Their mother had died so it was just he and the children. They got worried so they came to us to tell about it and the neighbors got together and they knew which direction he had gone. So they spread out in the woods and finally found him towards evening.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And they said, did you know you were lost? No, he said, I wasn't lost, I was here all the time.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But other than that, we'd go hunting so often that we knew all the hills and valleys around there pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went hunting, could you get around in the woods really easily or did you stick to trails?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, it was open enough so we would take a route to where we thought there were deer. If we found fairly good tracks, we would spread out and work around until we found them. Sometimes we didn't find them. If we were on the wind side and they got our scent, they'd take off and maybe land miles away.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So that is after hunting had gone on a while, they began to get more wild, you know, and more careful about where they roamed. But the first few years, the deer were not frightened because nobody had been in there to do any hunting much. So you'd go out and wait somewhere and pretty soon there might be a deer come walking by or maybe two or three of them. Other times, why, you'd go walk around until you jumped one or two and then you'd take time and sneak up on them, get them.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And there was no season at that time you would go out and hunt anytime you wanted to.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There were some cougars around. The deer would come down from up in the mountains when the snow got deep and the cougars would follow. But then when it got settled up and there were dogs around and that, why, the cougars stayed back more in the background.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was only once the cougar got into our yard we had butchered and we hung the meat up in the woodshed to freeze during the night. I think this was in cold weather and a cougar had strayed in there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I went over to a neighbor's. A few of us young fellows spent the evening there playing cards and visiting. I didn't have a gun with me at that time. I went down there horseback. When I came back in the evening or in the night, the dog met me over at the barn and he was so frightened that I suspected something was wrong. So as soon as I had the horse tied and fed, I started to the house and the dog followed me so close that he was actually bothering me from walking almost. When I came around to the gate by the house, I walked around to the front end of the cellar and woodshed. I spied the cougar. He was standing among some berry bushes we had there. He didn't leave but he stood there watching me.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I walked on to the house. As soon as I got in the house, I turned the light on. My folks had gone to bed. I turned the light on and went and got my rifle. When I got out, he had left there and he was over on the field at some distance from the house. He stopped and stood there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was moonlight enough so I could see him fairly plain but I couldn't see the sights on my gun so I didn't hit him when I fired at him. Of course, then he took off for the woods.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Next morning when I got out there, I could see his tracks around and around the woodshed. He could smell that meat in there. That's why he was there, trying to get in there but the door was closed so he couldn't get in. But he didn't come back neither.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was one instance during the very first days that people lived in there. One man went back in the woods to cut down a tree for shakes, to make shakes. It wasn't very far so he didn't bother to take a gun with him. He was by himself. He cut a tree down and he started to cut off some blocks at the foot of the tree.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: After a while he looked up and there was a cougar laying over by the top of the tree, laying right on the tree watching him. Well, he stood there a while to wonder what to do. He had had his lunch and he had a lunch pail there. This lunch pail was empty. So he picked up a stick and started pounding on that lunch pail. The cougar didn't move. So he started walking towards the cougar a little bit. He figured that with that stick he could probably fend himself. He started walking towards the cougar and pounding on that lunch pail. Finally the cougar went away. A cougar is an odd animal. He is not an animal that will actually tackle anybody unless he is either very hungry or is injured. So if you are bold enough to kind of stand up to him, he's apt to go away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when you were back in the woods away from Park going around the countryside, was it still the virgin timber there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes, it was. There was no timber cut for market until the potlatch company built a railroad in there. That was long about, I'd say somewhere between 1910 and 1915. We had moved away from there, sold our place and moved to Central Ridge on the Nesbirsh country then. So we weren't over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like? What was the timber like around? Not in Park itself but in the hills and the mountains and the backcountry there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was mostly yellow pine and fir. The fir timber grew mostly in higher land, drier land. The yellow pine grew almost anywhere there. Then further back there was white pine and some cedar and tamarack.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And of course after the potlatch company had took out what timber they had there, and they bought timber from the farmers there. So then there was mostly smaller trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was anybody living in this backwoods country then?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There were still several on the valley there, but further back there wasn't very many. There were several that went and filed on land under the Stone and Timber Act. And they didn't have to clear much land. I think they were required to clear up one acre of land. And they had to live on there a certain length of time. I don't remember just how much. And then after they proved up, they would sell the timber to the potlatch company. And it would be logged off.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't remember how many years the company logged back in there. But after they finished their logging, then they took the rails up off the track there and the land went back to pasture and such like. And of course through the valley where they had built a railroad, when that was tore up, they began to use the land again.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: From what you say, this whole great extent of land around Park, especially to the east I take it, was still almost untouched, had never been used for anything.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, there was enough timber back there that there wasn't any of the land used for farming, you know. It would just be open range for cattle and such.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were people running cattle back in there in the very early days?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't believe there was very many. We didn't get over there very often, so I don't know just how they managed back in there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There were some meadows there. Some of that was farmed and some of it was just kept for hay. They had a few cattle back there. But after we moved away, I never went back in that part, so I don't know just how they managed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you would go back hunting and horseback riding like you said that you did when you were getting older, would you find much sign of people having been there before? Were there old campfires that had been used?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Not very much. It was a very bad road back in there. You could get through, but you couldn't haul much of a load through there from the valley. So after the potlage company moved out, I don't know what they did. But while we lived in there, there was some mining done back in the Elk River country before the potlage went in there. And for a few years, I used to haul supplies back in there in the fall and winter. And there would be miles and miles where there was no people living at all. You had to go before the snow got deep. And you had to carry some of the necks and things with you in case there was a tree falling across the road. So it was a lonesome drive. It took about a day and a half to get in there. And I could come back in a day.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: As a rule, they knew that there would be horses brought in there bringing the freight in so they would have a little hay on hand. So I could stay there, in there, to rest the horses before I go back.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: At the very last, there were just two brothers. One of them was married. They were pleasure mining back in there, but they stayed through the winter. And because they wanted to be there when the snow began to melt, so they had high water. They had built up dams, so they had water pressure to slush the dirty way to clear for the placer mining. So I would stay with them a few days when I go back in there. They would always have some hay there. And I brought oats with me for the horses. So we visited that in a while. Later on, they moved out of there. They told me that they could take out about $500 worth of gold each spring from their pleasure mine. They'd take that out and sell it. Gold was worth about $35 an ounce then. Now, of course, it's many times that much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember their name or what they called their mine back there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Their name was Leslie, the surname. They were Russians. They had mined in B .C. a while before they came into Idaho. And somehow they had heard about this Elk River country and they went back there. They were possibly around 30 years old or something like that when they came in there. Very nice people. They treated me just very good when I was there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Could you... you don't know the name of it. They didn't have a name for that mine that they used.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. They didn't build up anything except just the dam that they built for slushing the water out. Otherwise, it just had their home there, you know. I don't know if they had a name for it or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about which creek it was on?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. There was two streams of water. They filed on 40 acres there. And these streams came down two separate drawers or outlets. And there was enough water. They had a four -inch hose for slushing the water out. And they had lots of force on that so they could cut down quite a bank of stone and soil there. Then they would finish up with a miner's pen, you know. They showed me how they did that. And I came in there before the water was very high and the water trickling down. They put their pen over there and showed me how they would work the stone and the water and work the gold to the bottom. But when the water was high, they had slush boxes then, you know. They would shovel the rocks and stuff into that and work it out. But I took the pen and worked and I happened to get a little gold in there and got a few tiny little kernels of gold, you know. I took that home with me and I kept it in a little bottle for years and years. But I don't know what happened to it. I lost it some way. That was quite a lot of work, doing that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You said that the first guys, those two men that came into the park country and lived there, were also miners who decided to locate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know any more about why they, had they been actually mining in park or were they passing through there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There were two men that first came to park. They were probably in their twenties or somewhere along in that. They were looking for minerals. They were miners. They walked across the Potlatch Canyon in the park and they couldn't find any good leads for minerals. And they liked the looks of that land so well that they decided to homestead there. So they went back out and to get their file down the land and bought some things to set up a home, you know. But they had to carry that across. There were no roads at all then. They were big husky men. One was a Norwegian and the other one was a Finnish Swede from the northern part of Sweden. And I remember them telling that they had to carry their cook stoves in. They bought very small stoves and carried them on their back across that canyon. And then they had access and saws and things to build cabins with. I can remember seeing the cabin of one of them. It was just a little bit of a room that he built at first, you know. Then he built a bigger one. And they located there. So when we came into the park, the road that we came in on led right by, close by, the cabin of one of those miners. They were pretty close to the breaks of the Potlatch Canyon. So where we lived was further back in the valley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they become farmers like the other people there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes, they cleared up land and exchanged work with the others that settled close to them there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were they about when you came in? About how old were they?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I'd say they would be somewhere near twenty -five years or so. They looked quite young.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I'd like you to tell me a little more about the exchange work.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I get the idea that there was a lot more exchange work than any other kind of work being done there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, being in the woods there, they had to have more help to clear away and build, you know. After they got houses built and barns and such like, then each would work on his own place then.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And of course, they were all, or practically all, were family people, so they soon had boys growing up to help at home. So then they didn't do so much exchange work.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You mentioned to me that there was once a time when there was a man who was supposed to do some exchange work for your father, and he fell through on it, and that was a really, it was just a bad thing for him to do. Remember you said there was a time when this guy that was supposed to do some work for your father, some exchange work for your father, and he reneged on it, he wouldn't do it. And you said you had to go over and tell him he was going to do it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes. Well, he was pretty much for himself and didn't seem to neighbor much with the others. Father had helped him, was going to exchange with him. I was about grown up at that time, and I went out on the prairie to work in the harvest fields. Father managed pretty well at home for a while, while I was away. And we had just a few acres that we could plant wheat on for threshing. Part of it we kept for hay, you know. And father didn't have a binder yet because there wasn't enough land broke to justify buying machinery like that, so he exchanged with his neighbor. And I happened to come home just about the time this wheat was to be cut, and his neighbor had a binder. So father had worked for him, and he had promised to cut this wheat for father. When the time came to cut it, he seemed to be messing with something else.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So I decided I was going to do something about it, so I went down there and had a little talk with the man and told him what would happen if he didn't come and cut the wheat. He agreed to come, and he came that afternoon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I take it that that was the exception to the rule and most all the time people took help?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, we had a nice neighborhood there, and good neighbors.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Later on we had time enough so we could build a church in there. One of our neighbors was a fairly good carpenter, so we had a meeting, got together, formed a committee, and made an agreement that this carpenter would do the finishing work on the building, and the rest of us around there would donate a certain amount of work on putting up the framework and that. So we went to work and had enough money we could buy the lumber and what was needed for it otherwise without hurting ourselves. So we went to work. My father and I agreed to lay the foundation. It was to be a rock foundation. So we hauled in rocks there, and father had learned a little about stonework so he could build a pretty good foundation. I was able to help him of course. So we hauled rocks over there and leveled up the ground and laid the rock foundation. Then the others went to work on the framework.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Soon they had the church up and the roof formed. But the steeple had to wait a while. They had stood there about a year or so before we built the steeple. I had moved away by that time over to Central Ridge. But I came back over to park and I helped them to put the steeple up. Then it took about another year or so before they hung a bell in the steeple. I had heard about that they were going to do that. I had a brother and family living there so I went back to visit them in the winter. So I got in on that too. I helped to hang the bell there, which I was happy to do because that was still the old stomping grounds where I used to live.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So then they had their meetings in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So about when was that church built? It was just before you left, huh?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That would be along about 1908 or 9 because we moved away in 1910.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Norwegian Lutheran?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes. It was a Lutheran community so this was a Lutheran church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before the church had been built, did people get together in the schoolhouse?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, we used that both for school and Sunday school and church. We had Christmas trees in there. Father was always delegated to bring the Christmas tree when we had our Christmas doings. I would help to put the trimmings on.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They would bring a pretty big tree. It would reach almost to the ceiling of the schoolhouse. They hadn't stood it out just how to set that tree, to brace it and so on. So Father got a good idea and he went home and he had a big two inch auger. They brought that over and they moved the desk over to one side and they bore the hole through the floor. Stuck the tree down through that hole in the floor.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Then they fastened the top to the ceiling so it couldn't fall down. So every year after that when Christmas time came, they'd pull the desk over to one side and stick the Christmas tree down through that hole in the floor.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We made up a lot of... we used to string popcorn and tinsel and things like that and hang on the tree. And presents, of course. We bought candles. There was no electricity there. So we bought candles and put on and lit them up. The tree looked pretty nice. And then had the program, of course, and handed out the presents. Then we would leave that tree there until after New Year. So there would be New Year's services there and everybody that hadn't been there for the Christmas doing could still see the tree. The last year we were there, the church was built then so we had our Christmas doings in the church.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Somebody got the idea that we should put up two trees in the church. So we did. And most of us that had helped with the trees in the schoolhouse were still living there. So we got together and put up two trees and we built that up with decorations to look real nice. We had tinsel and we strung wires across and we made up letters to string across. So we had an arch built. On the arch we had the words, welcome. And on the string we had the word, Merry Christmas strung out. And on the wall back of the trees we covered the walls with cedar boughs and tinsel. And we put up a big lamp there in a sort of a box that was open in the back and front. And in the back of there we had a big star made out of tinsel and a light that shone direct on that star. That was the Bethlehem Star. And the wall then was covered with cedar boughs and there were three windows on each side and in those windows were candles. And over the windows were boughs, cedar boughs, made into a sort of a, you might call it a a yuletree or something like that. And then we had more branches left and we cut them into small parts and covered the aisle on the floor so that the floor was like a carpet of green boughs.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And of course those trees and all that was left until New Year's so people that didn't see it before and those that did see it wanted to come back and see it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what was in the celebration itself? Do you remember what went on in the celebration?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we had a little program made up, you know, of songs and recitations and the minister had his Christmas message, you know. It wasn't so much but still it was what we were used to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the service in Norwegian or in English?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was in Norwegian, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In general, did the people in part, just everyday conversation, did they talk mostly Norwegian or mostly English?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was mostly Norwegian. There were two, there were three Swedish bachelors that lived there but of course they understood Norwegian as well.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was one family of seventy Adventists but they came to our services too so there was no friction there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any customs that are Norwegian customs that were kept in park that would be a little different from some of the other places?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, they had back east as well as back in Norway they had a feast on the fourth day of Christmas. It was either the twelfth or the thirteenth day that they would get together and have a good dinner together.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And there was on the fourth day of Christmas, that was the children's day, an old custom. We kids would be sort of honored that way and we would choose what we wanted to eat and all that. They would sort of wait on us then, which was a big thing for us, you know. Then the thirteenth day was just getting together for a visit and a dinner together. Everybody brought like a,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: they have nowadays when they have what they call a potluck dinner, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On this fourth day, that was the children's day, that mean it was a holiday for the kids?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, oh yes, we children didn't have any work to do that day. We were honored.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there other holidays, any others besides Christmas that were really traditional Norwegian type of celebration?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, we had the Fourth of July of course, like the others, and then there were certain holiday in church, you know, that they kept.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me about the way that the naming worked for your family? You told me that story and that seemed like a Norwegian kind of tradition. How the Rinsagers became the Swensens and the other way around.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, when my folks came from Norway, Father had to apply for citizenship in the States and his full name was Ivor Swenson Rinsage. Or in Norwegian they pronounce it Rinsaker. Well, when he took out his citizenship,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: that was in the court where they spoke English, of course, and they didn't like that Norwegian Rinsager. They couldn't pronounce it very clear, so they talked him into leaving that off and just have his name Ivor Swenson. So from then on, that was our full name, except in Minnesota they settled in a Norwegian settlement and we were known there as the Rinsager family. But legally it was just Ivor Swenson.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But when my oldest brother moved away, he was in a settlement where there was another Swenson and their mail was sometimes mixed up and he always liked the old Rinsager name. So he went to court and had his name changed to Rinsager. That's why my nephews here now are known as Ringsage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the name Rinsager comes from the province or the county?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It's from the district, just like the counties are here but in a smaller space, you might say. Each district had a separate name.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The place where father was born was Rinsager, so of course he carried that name when he came to the states.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Now there were some of the other neighbors. For instance, there was one named Kaaen, K -a -a -e -n. That was another district in Norway. Sunby, S -u -n -b -y, was another district. So those names were easy to pronounce in English so they just kept the... Torgeson was another name. Hellerud was another name from Norway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they feel about the kids learning English in school? Did they want the kids to learn English and speak English as quickly as they could? Did they want the kids to learn English and speak English as fast as they could in school?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, our first experience in English was when we started going to school. Schoolhouse was built so a year after we came to Idaho, we had a schoolhouse and we had to learn English in the school as much as we did at home. But for young folks, it don't take long. But even then, when we were at home, we'd talk Norwegian because our parents didn't talk much English. Father had learned enough in his dealings with people back in Minnesota. He had learned to speak a little English, enough to make himself understood. But mother didn't learn much. So naturally, we'd talk Norwegian when we were at home. When we kids were together, sometimes we'd talk Norwegian and sometimes English when we were out by ourselves. But in school, we had to learn to read English and talk English.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The first teacher we had was very good to us. He was very good. During recess, he was just like one of the kids. He'd play out there with us and have good times. That helped us so much. We liked the teachers, so naturally we liked to learn the English and do our studies in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He couldn't speak Norwegian though, your teacher, could he?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, he didn't know a word Norwegian. And maybe it was good because we had to learn to talk English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came into Troy, did you have any trouble with the kids in Troy who were from different places and had never spoken Norwegian?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, the man that built one of the first stores there was a Norwegian. The settlement east of Troy was mostly Swedish. So of course, Swedish and Norwegian is almost the same. So there was no trouble there. Eventually, a baracksmith started to shop there. He was Swedish. Most of the town of Troy was Swedish and Norwegian. So there was no trouble there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was a general merchandise store later on in Troy named the Olsen, Johnson, and Nelson. Three men that went together to organize that. So you see, Troy was pretty much a Swedish town.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The first bank in Troy, I don't remember just what nationality he was because I was just a youngster then. The second bank was organized by a man named Ole Bowman. He was a Swedish man. He was there not so long ago when he retired. By that time, Frank Brocke was one of the main clerks there. He took over and became the president of the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you describe to me what the saloons were like in Troy when you were a youngster or a young man going to town?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, they were not as bad as some places used to be. At first, it was mostly local people that patronized the saloons. They would get a little top -heavy once in a while and have a few arguments. When the potlatch company began to build a road into Elk River, then there were transients coming in.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: One of the livery barns in Troy organized a transportation rig, either wagon or sleigh, and they would take people up to the camps up in the woods. People had come in on the train and then they'd take them in. As a rule, there would be some coming out that wanted to quit.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: A man named Thompson had a hotel and one of the saloons connected with the hotel. He would keep money on hand so that when these men came from the woods after banking hours, he could pass their checks.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The saloon was next door, so they would go in there and spend some of their money. In the back room, they had the card tables going. When they got a few drinks, they had to go and play cards, of course. Some of them went there. Once in a while, the rest of the money would be delivered to someone in the back alley after they were knocked out.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But it wasn't bad. There were three saloons there at one time, so they went from one to the other.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was kind of rough on the policeman there because he was alone to kind of keep the streets clear. He had quite a job, but they managed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's a policeman that you're talking about, Marshall Hayes?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes. He got in trouble with a family man that lived in Troy.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't remember his name now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would be Paine Sly, the man that shot him. His name was Sly.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't remember just what it all started about, but this man Sly had made a remark that someday he was going to get even with that marshal.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So, finally the marshal went up there to arrest this man. He didn't have a search warrant with him, but he started to enter the house to take this man under arrest. So the man shot him.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He didn't quite get back to the streets in town until he went down and he bled to death. Well, then Hayes had two or three grown -up sons there in Troy, so they went on the warpath. They were going to get this man that shot Hayes. But they didn't dare go up to his house. They shot at the house. This man stepped out and told them, he said, there are two more men that I'm going to get and they're here and he started shooting. But he didn't get them neither. So, finally he died down and this man was arrested and served a few years in the penitentiary. But later the rumor was that he didn't come back to Troy. He settled somewhere else. I can't remember just where.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Marshall Hayes yourself? What kind of a guy did you think he was?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, what little I saw of him. He kind of had to grudge against the people in Troy because sometimes he had his hands full. Of course, he didn't have any charge except drunk and disorderly and for that he couldn't shoot a man.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He was kind of grouchy, but they put up with it until after he was shot while they got other men in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think he got worse as the years went on?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, he couldn't exactly do as he liked. He had to stay within the law too, you know. A peace officer isn't free to do just what he would like to do. He has to do what the law says he should do. So he had to stay within reason. So they just managed. Then after he died, why, they got a younger man in there. By that time we had moved over on the reservation so I didn't get to Troy very often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you talk to him ever, by the way? Did you ever talk to Marshall Hayes?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Very little because he was a stranger to me. When I did go to Troy, I didn't stay there very long, those times. I managed to stay sober so I didn't have any trouble with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel that you had to sort of watch yourself when you were in the saloons in Troy?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There were times I was down there that they celebrated, but I didn't do any drinking so I kind of kept to myself. I'd go in and have a glass of beer and sometimes it would be quite crowded and noisy in there, but I didn't stay there to celebrate so I had no trouble with anybody. I could see what was going on and there were times when if anybody got a little noisy, why, he'd better stay away from that crowd. It was very dry.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And of course then it wasn't very long until the state went dry so the saloons were closed up. Family men were kind of like out in the country and in town. They hated the saloons because a woman walking down the street might meet a bunch of drunk fellows, you know, and that wasn't very nice. And things like that was what helped to form petitions to get the state dried, to get the saloons abolished.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who do you think most of the people were that were drinking in the saloons? Were they local guys or were they mostly from the camps?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, at first it was local men, but when the camps were built back there, then at certain days they would come in, but they wouldn't stay long. They'd celebrate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were these guys that were rolling those drunk fellows back in the alleys? I mean, who were these guys that they were drinking with, I wonder?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I'd just heard about that. I never was there to see or to know any of these men. They were local men, of course, that would watch for those chances.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And there wasn't so very much of that neither because it got to be known around, you know, and people coming in there to have drinks, why, they'd kind of look out for themselves pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were often groups of guys who came in together from the woods?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, they came in and after they had celebrated a while, they'd get on the train and go out to other places, people that roamed around from one job to another, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned to me once about a time when you had to take care of a guy that was drunk and he didn't appreciate it very much?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That was after the, well, just about the time that the railroad was built through there. I went up there to do some carpenter work and there were two saloons there just built along the road, rough house. And a sawmill was located about three miles from there. And those men were hauling lumber to a market up on the other railroad. And they could make a trip in a day easily. And there was two men that started stopping in for drinks when they came back with the empty wagon.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And they were hauling from that same sawmill. One of them started back home while he was fairly sober, but the other one stayed a little longer. And when he came out through the door, he wasn't able to manage his team, so the saloon men came out and asked me if I would take him home, which I did. And there was no wagon box on the wagon when they hauled lumber. They just had the running gears of the wagon. So I had to sit on the back reach of the wagon and kind of hold him while I drove the horses. And he wasn't a bad man, but when we got out of West he felt that he wanted more whiskey. So he wanted to go back, so I had to stop and kind of have a conversation with him and get him cooled down and get him back on the wagon again. And drive away, so we stopped two or three times before we got out to the sawmill. And then he began to half sober up and he got mad at me for taking him home. When he got off of the wagon, he said, when I get to the cabin, he said, I'll fix you. He said, I've got a rifle up there. At that time I was talking with the man that was operating the mill there. And I told some of the men up at the bunkhouse to take care of that man because I said if he comes back out, and I had my hand in my hip pocket. I said, if he comes out with a gun, I said it's going to be too bad for him. So they grabbed him and took him back in the bunkhouse. I got through talking with the man that run the sawmill, I went home. I brought a saddle horse along for me to ride back. Next day when he came through there, he came over and apologized to me. He said he was sorry that he caused me any trouble. And he seemed to be a nice, decent man when he was sober. So he caused no more trouble. He didn't get that drunk anymore at that place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What place was this? Was this out on the Harvard -Derry Cut -Off area where they were putting the train through?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I forget the name of that place where they hauled to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say they had put up little shacks and saloons just as the road was going through so the men would have a place to drink, huh?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I built a bar in there. He had only a couple boards nailed on the wall at first, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And they built a railroad camp not far from there. So on Saturday night the men would come in there to tank up, you know. So I had to stay there in case there was any trouble. And there was another man there. He had been a blacksmith, a young man. He was a pretty husky man. So if it looked like it was going to be too much for me to handle them, why, they'd call on him. And one night we found out afterwards that one night these men, I think there was five of them, they had come down with the intention of robbing the saloon. He didn't have a safe for his money, you know. He just kept them in a box there. So they came in and they'd walk up to the bar and take a drink. Then they'd stand around the heater there, this was in cold weather. For a while then they'd go up to the bar and take another drink. But they didn't talk very much. And this saloon keeper, he began to understand that there was something brewing, so he sent for me. I came in there and I'd walk up to the bar with him, but I'd have my drink coming, you see, because I didn't drink. Then they began to get a little more talkative as the whiskey took effect. Finally they began to have a little trouble among themselves. Didn't amount to much. But they kept on drinking. They had one of these sheet iron heaters in there. Two of them finally began to battle a little bit and they tore that heater down. So I picked that up and threw it outside. There was a back room there. I came through that room and there I saw that blacksmith standing with a full quart bottle in each hand for a weapon. But he stayed back in the dark so long as he wasn't needed. He stayed back there because the idea was not to cause any trouble so long as these men didn't get too bad. Because they were drinking and the bartender was making money, you know. Finally it got pretty rough. So there was a man and woman came in there and one of these fellas started talking bad to the woman. She wasn't no bashful woman either and she turned around and kind of started to hit him. So I took him and put him up against the wall and she walked up and hit him a couple of times in one eye and blacked his eye for him. By that time another man grabbed her and shoved her out through the door. Her man didn't dare to do anything.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Then they went up to the bar again and there was one young fellow with him. I guess it was getting towards the time they wanted to rob the bank, you know. He got his pocket knife out, a big pocket knife he had, and held that in his hand.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So I sneaked up behind him. He didn't notice me and I kept quiet until he started to raise his hand like he was going to strike at the saloon keeper. I brought my arm down so if he had started strike I would have held his arm. Then that fellow back in the storeroom, he came in. Then we put him out. They all went out and went away to the other saloon. With that bartender he was so cool. He stood with one elbow up on the bar and his back partly turned towards the man that had the knife.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But he didn't do a thing until we came in and then we shoved him outside to live.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They didn't come back that night but they started a little trouble over at the other place. Somebody came and called on us to come over there. So we went over there but they didn't cause much trouble there. Just as we came towards the doorway, one of them came out, landed first in the snowbank. But when we came in there they seemed to understand that there was more help there so they quieted down and then they went back to camp. After that there was no trouble there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did he think, or why did he know he could call on you to help?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well his saloon was only just a few yards from there, you know. So he knew us there and he sent a man over to get us.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You were working as a carpenter on the… I was there probably a month's time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you carpentering for the camps?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. Two men started a livery set up there. A livery barn. I built that livery barn and did that work in the saloon there. And I put up a shack for one of those fellas that was going to run the livery barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that near Avon by any chance?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was a little over a mile, two miles maybe from Avon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this change the country very much? The coming of the actual…when the railroad was coming through did it really make…?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yeah, they began to haul timber to the railroad then, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Several of the farmers up there had quite a lot of timber standing yet on their land so they hired help and started logging it to the railroad. And that got their land cleared up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There are a couple things about Troy that I wanted to ask you that I didn't yet. One is about how often would you go into town from Park?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, as the road got improved a little more we would go maybe once a month or so. But we had no reason to go to town much except to get supplies, you know. And there were no cars at those times. To drive twenty miles with a team and wagon, that was no pleasure so we didn't go very often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were there in the early days, what do you remember the town looking like?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The streets were just dark. They didn't have any gravel or any covering for the road so when the rain and snow came, it was pretty rough. We had enough snow so when winter came they'd have good roadbed with snow. But when that broke up then there was pretty deep mud there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And there weren't very many buildings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There weren't very many buildings in the town then?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But after they began to get better roads, the town built up pretty fast. They had what I can remember, they must have had a population of a thousand or more. They had two livery barns and two blacksmith shops, a lumber yard, sawmills close by.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I don't remember just how many department stores they had. I remember one, the one I mentioned a little while ago. I think it was two hardware stores and one or two hotels.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And when they began to have cars, why there were service stations built up right away. And the livery barns began to disappear. And there wasn't so many horses.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And they built up warehouses.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But we moved away in 1910 so Troy hadn't built up a great deal at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you sold the place, how much did you have cleared roughly?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, about 40 -50 acres. I rented some land so that kind of slowed down on the work at home. I rented a farm nearby there so I had work to do there much of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the place you said was 80 acres, is that right?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's because your father had already homesteaded?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, he homesteaded 80 acres in Minnesota and then another 80 over at Park. And that was, much of that was quite heavy timbered so we just kept that for pasture and worked on the more open land. And there was no market for timber while we lived there so we didn't have a chance to sell any of the timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you decide to leave Park area?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, my oldest brother homesteaded on what they call Central Ridge. That was prairie land. And he would have liked to have us come over there. And then finally one of his neighbors decided to sell his farm and that joined with my brother's place. And the land wasn't so high priced then. So I went over there to look at it and talked with the owner and he decided he would sell it to me for a certain amount down. I think it was $2 ,500 down and the total price on that land was only $3 ,500. So I could see a fairly good chance of making that. So I bought it on a contract to pay down a certain amount each year. And my oldest brother and I talked over about the place Father had over at Park and we didn't like to sell to a stranger. And my brother and I made a deal that he bought it from us. Well, I kind of had the charge of the place then. And so he paid down a fair amount on that and I went and paid that down on the land I bought on Central Ridge and we moved over there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I was there about five years. The first year I lost 30 acres of beans that froze and that didn't help much and I began to get discouraged about farming. I had heard about land up in Canada that was to be bought cheap and also that there was rangeland for cattle up there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So my brother and I and my folks made arrangements that they would live with him and I would sell my place and move to Canada.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So I was there about eight years and where I located wasn't, it was good farming land but the climate wasn't very good. It would either freeze out or dry out some years. So we decided to move back to the States and sold there. But I didn't do any more farming after that. I worked mainly on carpenter work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you feel about leaving Park when you left?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I wasn't really happy about it because that's where I grew up, you know. That had been my home all those years. But I could see a better life ahead over yonder so we decided to move.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But I went back there to visit my brother and neighbors there every year while we lived on Central Ridge. I spent many good days back there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had Park changed very much by the time that you left? I mean I know it got built up while you were growing up but had the area changed? Had the people changed a lot?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, several of them sold out and moved away from there. And I didn't get very well acquainted with the new people that came in because I wasn't over there very often. So it began to change. Then at a certain time after the potlatch company had logged the land off, more people wanted to sell. And there was a businessman named Powell in Moscow that bought up some of that land. And another party bought up other parts there and made bigger farms of it. And that meant fewer people over there. So now there aren't very many living there anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any feeling about when you think the best times were for Park, for the community?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, things didn't change much, you know. So we grew up with the land being settled more. But I was never satisfied really to be there because there didn't seem to be much of a future there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So when we moved away from there, I began to lose interest, except to go back there and visit with my brother who lived there and the old -timers that were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you feel that there wasn't much of a future?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we had only a small farm there and it would have taken ages to make all of that into farmland. And there was no sale for timber at the time we moved away from there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I rented some farmland there and that was enough to keep us going, but I couldn't see any chance to improve much on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any story about the community that we didn't get down?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And that was about them building a lot of houses at once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that they got together and built maybe six, seven houses at once in the early days?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, that was in the earlier days. That was the time that my father built the second house, or what we still call the new house, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Most of the settlers in there were men that were used to working with timber, so they were good at that kind of work. And they were doing exchange work and so they talked over that about five of them wanted to build a new house and they agreed to work together, exchange work. So when one of them was ready to build, the others would come and they would build that up to the top plate to where they would put the roof on. Then they would go to the next one and so on around. They all had their logs ready. So they built up those five houses in one summer between spring work and harvest. And then each one would hire somebody to help put the roof on. That meant carpenter work. One of our neighbors was a carpenter and he came and helped with the roof on our house. Did the measuring and cutting of the rafters and such like.
